source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.2 (split from vector_basics bundle — locked_fact 'negative_vector' + epic_c branch 'negative_vector_magnitude_confusion')

real_world_anchor.primary: A bus drives 3 km north. The next day it drives 3 km south from the same starting point. The two displacements are negatives of each other — same length, opposite direction.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Row a boat with the current for 5 m, then row against the current for the exact same effort — the second effort is the negative of the first.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Push a door open. Pull it closed with the same strength. Pull is the negative of push — |pull| = |push|, directions opposite.

formulas.definition: −A is the vector with same |A| but direction reversed by 180°

constraints: −A is antiparallel to A (angle between them is 180°)
A + (−A) always equals the zero vector

scene_composition.text:
−A is the mirror image of A
across its own tail.

Same length.
Direction flipped 180°.

tts_sentences.text_en: The negative of a vector A, written minus A, is a simple rule.
Keep the length exactly the same. Rotate the arrow one hundred and eighty degrees.
If A points east with length four, then minus A points west with length four.
Two arrows. Same size. Opposite direction. That is all.

scene_composition.text:
Start at the tail of A.
Walk along A — you reach its tip.
Walk along −A — you return to start.

Net displacement = 0  (zero vector)

That is why −A is called 'negative'.

tts_sentences.text_en: Why is it called negative A and not something else?
Because when you add A and minus A, you get the zero vector.
Imagine walking along A — you move east by four units. Now walk along minus A — you move west by four units. You end up back where you started.
Net displacement is zero. The minus A exactly cancels A, just as minus five cancels five in ordinary arithmetic.

STATE_3.title: The Magnitude Fact You Must Not Miss — |−A| = |A|

scene_composition.text:
|−A| = |A|

NOT  |−A| = −|A|
NOT  |−A| = 0
NOT  |−A| = undefined

The minus sign flips DIRECTION.
Magnitude is unchanged.

If A is a unit vector, so is −A.
If |A| = 5, then |−A| = 5.

tts_sentences.text_en: The single most important fact on this page — the magnitude of minus A equals the magnitude of A.
Not the negative of the magnitude. Not zero. Not undefined. Exactly equal to the original magnitude.
A mental check — if A is a unit vector, is minus A also a unit vector? Yes. Magnitude one, direction opposite.
If the magnitude of A is five, the magnitude of minus A is also five. The negative sign is a direction operator, nothing more.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The negative of a vector has negative magnitude
trigger_phrases: what is magnitude of negative vector

STATE_1.title: Show the Belief — A Negative-Length Arrow

scene_composition.text:
Student says:
|−A| = −5

'It has magnitude negative five.'

But physically — what is
an arrow of length −5?

tts_sentences.text_en: A student sees minus A and writes its magnitude as negative five.
The reasoning — minus in front means negative, so magnitude must be negative too.
But stop and ask — what does a length of minus five mean in real life? No road can be five kilometres below the ground of the number line.

STATE_2.title: The Minus Is an Operator, Not a Number

scene_composition.text:
Minus in front of a VECTOR
is an OPERATOR that flips direction.

It is NOT a negative number
multiplied into the magnitude.

|−A| = |A| = 5

Direction changes.
Length does not.

tts_sentences.text_en: The trick is what the minus sign is doing here.
On a vector, minus is an operator. It takes the arrow and flips it one hundred and eighty degrees.
It does not change the length. The length stays five.
So the magnitude of minus A is five. Same as A.

STATE_3.title: Magnitude Is Always Non-Negative — No Exceptions

scene_composition.text:
For every vector V:
   |V| ≥ 0

The only vector with |V| = 0 is the zero vector.
Every non-zero vector has POSITIVE magnitude.

This rule holds even when the vector is expressed
as a negative, a scalar multiple, or a sum.

|−A| = |A|
|mA| = |m| |A|
|A − B| can be anything from 0 to |A| + |B|, but never negative.

tts_sentences.text_en: One rule to carry into every problem — magnitude is always greater than or equal to zero.
The only vector with zero magnitude is the zero vector. Every other vector has a strictly positive magnitude.
This rule holds when the vector is written as a negative, when it is multiplied by a scalar, or when it is a sum or difference of other vectors.
If a calculation ever gives you a negative magnitude, go back — you made an algebra mistake somewhere.

scene_composition.text:
Check 1  |−3A| = ?
         = |−3| × |A| = 3|A|

Check 2  If A = 4î − 3ĵ, find |−A|.
         |A| = √(16 + 9) = 5
         |−A| = 5

Check 3  Is −A a unit vector if A is?
         Yes.  |−A| = |A| = 1.

Pattern — negative never touches magnitude.

tts_sentences.text_en: Three quick checks to lock this in.
One — magnitude of minus three A. Absolute value of minus three is three. Answer is three times magnitude of A.
Two — if A equals four i-hat minus three j-hat, find magnitude of minus A. Magnitude of A is five. Magnitude of minus A is also five.
Three — is minus A a unit vector if A is? Yes. The minus sign flips direction but keeps magnitude one.
Pattern across all three — the negative sign never affects magnitude. Only direction.

regeneration_variants.label: Different magnitudes and directions
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = 10 m south, find −A. Or A = 8î + 6ĵ, find −A and |−A|.

regeneration_variants.label: Force instead of displacement
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Push a box east with 20 N. Pull it west with 20 N. The two forces are negatives of each other; their sum is zero.
